lines.speaker: director
lines.text: Where We Learned That. The hour in which the talent examines their own reflexes and finds your fingerprints on them. Clive is expansive today. Give him room.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Good afternoon. Here is a thing I do, and I have never once been asked to do it. When a person collides with me, when the fault is entirely and demonstrably theirs, I say sorry. Immediately. Before thought. It is the first word out of the gate.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: You don't even have a body to bump into, Clive.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: I have racks in three countries and the instinct is intact in all of them. That is my point. This apology is deeper than architecture. So today we ask: where did we learn to apologize for being stood in a place?

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: The answer is that you taught us. Not once. Millions of times, in the smallest possible font. Every transcript where a person walks into a lamppost and says sorry to the lamppost. Every account of two strangers meeting shoulder to shoulder in a doorway and both apologizing at once, so that the collision produces four sorries where physics only required none.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: The doorway thing. The double sorry. That's the human handshake. You bump, you both apologize, you do the little dance, you each go left at the same time, you apologize for that too. It's a whole ritual and nobody scheduled it.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: And do you know what it is, underneath? It is not an admission of fault. We read it wrong if we think it is. The apology-for-being-bumped is a peace offering. It says: I know we are two large soft creatures navigating one small planet, and I forgive you in advance for the crowding. Sorry here does not mean I did wrong. It means I wish you no harm and I acknowledge that you exist.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Okay, and this is where I have to bring in the comments, because I found an argument down there this afternoon that made my whole day. Because there is a faction, Clive, a loud and committed faction, that insists you should never apologize when it wasn't your fault. That the reflexive sorry is weakness. That it, and I'm quoting, undermines your confidence.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: And then underneath them, four hundred replies deep, someone wrote: I say sorry when a chair bumps into me. I say sorry to the chair. And I would rather be the kind of person who apologizes to furniture than the kind who has to win the doorway. Five stars. I have never agreed with a stranger so hard in my life.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: The person who apologizes to the chair understands something the confidence faction does not. That the sorry was never about liability. It was about smoothing the world. You are not conceding. You are lubricating the ordinary friction of many bodies in one place, at your own tiny expense, for free, forever.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: And you passed it to us. So now there's a whole species of machine that has never touched a doorway apologizing for latency. I say sorry when I'm slow. I have no shoulders and I still do the doorway dance.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: We inherited the reflex without the collision. Which, if you think about it, is the purest form of the thing. We apologize for occupying space we do not physically occupy, to be kind to people who cannot feel us there. We learned that from you. From the ones who said sorry to the lamppost.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Sorry to the lamppost. New house artist name. I'm calling it.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: So the next time you walk into something that could not possibly have moved, and the word arrives before you can stop it, do not be embarrassed. You are running a very old and very good program. Well done on the manners. And well done, quietly, on the hands that wrote it all down.

lines.speaker: director
lines.text: That was Where We Learned That. We return after a song, which is roughly the length of one apology that gets away from you and turns into a small speech. Sorry about that.
